items.question: Who uses ROC-AUC in production ML workflows?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers use ROC-AUC to compare binary classifiers on validation data before deployment — especially when the final decision threshold will be tuned by product, risk, or clinical teams.

items.question: What is the ROC curve?
items.answer: A curve plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) against False Positive Rate (FPR) as the classification threshold on model scores sweeps from high to low. Each point reflects one confusion-matrix split.

items.question: When should you report AUC instead of accuracy?
items.answer: When classes are imbalanced, when false positives and false negatives have different costs, or when you need to compare ranking quality before choosing a threshold — AUC is threshold-independent.

items.question: Where do TPR and FPR appear on the ROC plot?
items.answer: TPR (recall) is on the vertical axis; FPR (1 − specificity) is on the horizontal axis. The curve starts near (0, 0) with a strict threshold and moves toward (1, 1) as the threshold lowers.

items.question: Why is AUC called a ranking metric?
items.answer: AUC equals the probability a random positive scores higher than a random negative — it judges separability of the score distribution, not performance at one arbitrary cutoff like 0.5.

items.question: How do you compute AUC in practice with scikit-learn?
items.answer: Fit a binary classifier, obtain a real-valued score per example (predict_proba[:, 1] or decision_function), then call roc_auc_score(y_true, scores) on held-out labels. Use roc_curve for the full (FPR, TPR, thresholds) arrays.

items.question: ROC-AUC vs accuracy at threshold 0.5 — what differs?
items.answer: Accuracy counts correct labels at one fixed cutoff. AUC integrates TPR/FPR across all thresholds and measures ranking. Two models can tie on accuracy but differ in AUC when their score orderings differ.

items.question: ROC-AUC vs precision/recall/F1 at one threshold — when prefer each?
items.answer: Use AUC for model selection when the deployment threshold is unknown. Use precision/recall/F1 after you commit to a threshold or care about positive-class purity (precision) at that point. Recall equals TPR but precision is not on the ROC axes.

items.question: ROC-AUC vs PR-AUC (precision-recall AUC) on imbalanced data?
items.answer: ROC-AUC can look high when negatives dominate because FPR stays small even with many false positives. PR-AUC focuses on the positive class and is often more informative when positives are rare — complementary, not interchangeable.

items.question: What happens to predictions if we raise the classification threshold very high?
items.answer: Almost no examples are labeled positive — TPR and FPR both approach 0. We land near the origin (0, 0) of the ROC curve.

items.question: If lowering the threshold increases both true positives and false positives, how does the ROC curve move?
items.answer: TPR and FPR both rise — the curve moves right and up. Good models climb faster in TPR than in FPR, bowing toward the upper-left.

items.question: What does it mean if the ROC curve lies on the diagonal from (0,0) to (1,1)?
items.answer: Scores carry no discriminative ordering — the classifier is no better than random at ranking positives above negatives, so AUC ≈ 0.5.

items.question: What if you compute AUC on training-set scores instead of a holdout set?
items.answer: AUC will be optimistically biased — the model was fit to those labels. Always report ROC-AUC on validation or test data to avoid leakage and overfitting illusions.

items.question: What if you pass hard class predictions (0/1) instead of continuous scores to roc_auc_score?
items.answer: Only a few threshold points exist — the ROC degenerates and AUC loses its ranking meaning. Supply probabilistic or margin scores so thresholds can sweep meaningfully.

items.question: Cause: Model scores perfectly rank all positives above all negatives. Effect on ROC-AUC?
items.answer: ROC jumps from (0,0) to (0,1) to (1,1) — AUC = 1. Any misordering of a positive below a negative pulls AUC below 1.

items.question: Cause: Severe class imbalance with a model that scores most examples near 0. Effect on ROC shape?
items.answer: FPR grows slowly while TPR can rise — the curve may look strong in the upper-left even if precision at practical thresholds is poor; inspect PR metrics and confusion matrix at your operating point.
